title: Biomagnification Through a Food Web
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Interactive trophic pyramid showing how pollutant concentrations magnify approximately 10x at each level with adjustable initial concentration

learningObjectives: Compare pollutant concentrations across trophic levels and explain why top predators are most affected
Calculate the approximate magnification factor between adjacent trophic levels
Predict when toxin concentrations will exceed danger thresholds
Connect biomagnification to real-world examples like DDT and mercury
prerequisites: Understanding of food webs and trophic levels
Knowledge of producer, consumer, and predator concepts
Familiarity with parts per million (ppm) as a concentration unit

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Slider-driven quantitative exploration with visual danger indicators
keyQuestions: Why are top predators most vulnerable to persistent pollutants?
What initial water concentration is truly 'safe' for the entire food web?
How does the 10x magnification factor explain why DDT nearly drove eagles to extinction?
Why do fish consumption advisories target large predatory fish like tuna and swordfish?
commonMisconceptions: If the water concentration is safe for humans, the food web is safe (biomagnification can create dangerous levels at higher trophic levels)
All pollutants biomagnify equally (only persistent, fat-soluble chemicals accumulate significantly)
Biomagnification only happens in aquatic ecosystems (it also occurs in terrestrial food webs)
assessmentOpportunities: Have students calculate concentrations at each trophic level for a given initial value
Ask students to determine the maximum safe initial concentration for top predators
Challenge students to explain why banning DDT helped eagle populations recover using the magnification model
